attributes.stem:
A 27-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic for routine antenatal screening at 12 weeks gestation in her first pregnancy. She has no significant medical history and takes only pregnancy vitamins.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. An ultrasound examination shows normal fetal development and notes the presence of gallstones. She has never experienced any abdominal pain, nausea, or other symptoms suggestive of gallstone disease. The patient asks what should be done about the gallstones.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management plan?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has asymptomatic gallstones discovered incidentally during routine antenatal ultrasound. The natural history of asymptomatic gallstones is generally benign, with only 1-4% of people developing symptoms annually. Therefore, no intervention is the most appropriate management plan.

Prompt laparoscopic cholecystectomy would expose the patient to unnecessary surgical risks during pregnancy. Surgery during pregnancy should be reserved for symptomatic gallstone disease that fails conservative management or complications such as cholecystitis.

Laparoscopic cholecystectomy in the puerperium is not indicated as the patient has no symptoms. Prophylactic surgery cannot be justified given the low risk of developing symptoms.

Annual ultrasound surveillance is unnecessary as it would not change management in the absence of symptoms. Regular monitoring of asymptomatic gallstones does not provide clinical benefit.

Monthly ultrasound surveillance until postpartum would be excessive and unnecessary. The presence of gallstones alone does not warrant increased monitoring during pregnancy.

Pregnancy is actually a risk factor for gallstone formation due to increased cholesterol secretion and reduced gallbladder motility from elevated progesterone levels. However, the presence of asymptomatic gallstones does not require any specific intervention or monitoring during pregnancy. The patient should be advised to seek medical attention if she develops symptoms such as right upper quadrant pain, particularly after fatty meals.
learningPoints: Asymptomatic gallstones do not require treatment or surveillance during pregnancy.
Only 1-4% of people with asymptomatic gallstones develop symptoms per year.
Pregnancy is a risk factor for gallstone formation due to increased cholesterol secretion and reduced gallbladder motility.
references: Sun H, Tang H, Jiang S, Zeng L, Chen E, Zhou T, Wang Y. Gender and metabolic differences of gallstone diseases. World J Gastroenerol. 2009;15(15):1886-1891. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2670418/

Thwayeb Y, Gonzalez N, Garcia R. Natural history of asymptomatic gallstones. A prospective 18-year follow-up. Eastern Journal of Medicine. 2004;9:57-62. Available at: https://jag.journalagent.com/ejm/pdfs/EJM_9_2_57_62.pdf

Lammert F, Acalovschi, Ercolani G, Erpecum K, Gurusamy K, Laarhoven C, Portincasa P. EASL Clinical Practice Guidelines on the prevention, diagnosis and treatment of gallstones. Journal of Hepatology. 2016;65:146-181. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27085810/

Bari O, Wang T, Paik C, Portincasa P, Wang D. Cholesterol cholelithiasis in pregnant women: pathogenesis, prevention and treatment. Annals of Hepatology. 2014;13(6):728-745. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25332259/
